input.background: Acute lower respiratory tract infection is a leading cause of death among infants and under-5s in developing countries. A simple test is needed to differentiate between infants with acute respiratory infection who have lower respiratory tract infection (LRI) and require antibiotics, and those with upper respiratory tract infection (URI). The study explores the usefulness of respiratory rate as a diagnostic tool for this purpose.
input.data:
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Respiratory rate (breaths/min) & Number of children (\%) \\
\hline & LRI & URI \\
\hline 0-30 & 1 (1\%) & 16 (11\%) \\
\hline 31-40 & 4 (3\%) & 77 (51\%) \\
\hline 41-50 & 10 (7\%) & 46 (30\%) \\
\hline 51-60 & 41 (29\%) & 9 (6\%) \\
\hline 61+ & 86 (61\%) & 3 (2\%) \\
\hline Total & 142 (100\%) & 151 (100\%) \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
input.question: Given that the prevalence of LRI among all infants with acute respiratory infection in a developing country is 3%, which cut-off gives the best balance of positive and negative predictive values (PPV and NPV) when the prevalence is 3%?

output.answer:
Using the formulae for PPV and NPV:

| Cut-off | PPV   | NPV   |
|---------|-------|-------|
| 30      | 3.3%  | 99.8% |
| 40      | 7%    | 99.8% |
| 50      | 26%   | 99.6% |
| 60      | 49%   | 98.8% |

With low prevalence, NPV is usually not helpful. The PPV increases as the cut-off level rises. The 'best' choice to maximize correct predictions is a cut-off of 60 breaths/min. However, with this cut-off, nearly half of the infants with LRI would be missed. A cut-off of 50 breaths/min would identify nearly all infants with LRI but result in more false positives compared to a cut-off of 60 breaths/min.
